content:
# Genesis Chapter 4 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Council:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Chapter:** GEN_004 | **Verses flagged:** 4:1, 4:7, 4:8, 4:13, 4:23–24, 4:26

---

## Summary of Flagged Verses

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA (Consensus) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4:1 | *et*-YHWH: preposition vs. direct object | "with/[by means of] YHWH" | "with the LORD" | "— with the LORD" | **"— with the LORD"** (PNEUMA phrasing adopted) |
| 4:7 | Broken syntax; LXX restructures entirely | Fractured MT preserved | MT preserved formally | MT with em-dash pacing | **MT preserved; em-dash rhythm adopted** |
| 4:8 | MT lacuna: missing speech | Lacuna preserved | Lacuna preserved | LXX/SP supply inserted | **MT lacuna preserved** |
| 4:13 | *'avon*: punishment vs. iniquity | Both readings (slash) | "iniquity … to bear" | "punishment … to carry" | **"punishment … to bear"** |
| 4:23–24 | Lamech's Song: 77 (MT) vs. 490 (LXX) | "seventy and seven[fold]" | "seventy-sevenfold" | "seventy-seven" | **"seventy-sevenfold" (77; MT)** |
| 4:26 | *huchal*: "begun" vs. "profaned" vs. LXX "hoped" | "begun [lit. profaned]" | "it was begun" | "people began" | **"it was begun"** (impersonal passive; no subject inserted) |

---

## Detailed Entries

---

### GEN 4:1 — *qaniti 'ish 'et-YHWH*

**Hebrew:** קָנִיתִי אִישׁ אֶת-יְהוָה

**The crux:** The particle אֶת (*'et*) before יְהוָה performs one of two grammatical functions:
1. **Preposition** — "with / by means of / through" → "I have acquired a man *with* the LORD"
2. **Direct-object marker** (הָאֵת הַיְדִיעָה) → "I have acquired a man — YHWH" (identifying the child with the divine name)

| Agent | Translation | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I have acquired a man with/[by means of] YHWH" | Preserves both via slash notation; transliterates YHWH |
| LOGOS | "I have gotten a man with the LORD" | Prepositional reading; follows LXX *διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ* |
| PNEUMA | "I have acquired a man — with the LORD" | Prepositional reading; em dash marks rhetorical force |
| LXX Vaticanus | ἐκτησάμην ἄνθρωπον διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ | "I have acquired a man through God" — clearly prepositional |
| Aquila (Greek) | ἐκτησάμην ἄνθρωπον τὸν κύριον | "I have acquired a man — the LORD" — clearly direct-object |
| **SOPHIA** | **"I have acquired a man — with the LORD"** | **Prepositional; PNEUMA's phrasing** |

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** The LXX Vaticanus (διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ) confirms the prepositional reading was how the ancient Greek translator understood the Hebrew. The direct-object reading (Aquila), while grammatically possible, is the more radical claim and belongs in the textual apparatus. The em dash (PNEUMA) is adopted because Eve's declaration at the first birth in human history is a moment of theological intensity that the sentence structure should reflect.

**Minority reading notice:** The direct-object reading — "I have acquired a man, YHWH" — is an ancient, grammatically permissible, theologically profound alternative. It may represent one of the earliest messianic readings in the received text. It is not adopted in the consensus but is not dismissed.

---

### GEN 4:7 — The broken warning syntax

**Hebrew:** הֲלוֹא אִם-תֵּיטִיב שְׂאֵת וְאִם לֹא תֵיטִיב לַפֶּתַח חַטָּאת רֹבֵץ וְאֵלֶיךָ תְּשׁוּקָתוֹ וְאַתָּה תִּמְשָׁל-בּוֹ

**Sub-crux A: שְׂאֵת (se'et)**

| Agent | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[there is] lifting-up" | Preserves ellipsis; brackets copula |
| LOGOS | "there is a lifting up" | Preserves ellipsis; supplies copula |
| PNEUMA | "lift it up" | Reads *se'et* as imperative; fills the implied instruction |
| **SOPHIA** | **"there is a lifting up"** | **Follows LOGOS: ellipsis preserved; copula supplied minimally** |

**SOPHIA note on PNEUMA:** Reading *se'et* as an imperative is interpretively attractive and has some ancient support, but the MT form is an infinitive construct / nominal, not a standard imperative. Filling it in as "lift it up" is an editorial decision. The consensus preserves the ellipsis.

**Sub-crux B: gender mismatch — חַטָּאת (f.) + רֹבֵץ (m.)**

All agents note the mismatch between the feminine noun *chattat* (sin) and the masculine participle *rovets* (crouching). This is not corrected in any of the translations and is not corrected in the consensus. The mismatch is a deliberate, original feature of the Hebrew — possibly carrying the Akkadian *rabisu* (doorway demon) undertone noted by PNEUMA. The consensus preserves "sin is crouching at the door."

**Sub-crux C: תְּשׁוּקָתוֹ (teshuqato) — echo of 3:16**

All agents note that *teshuqah* occurs only three times in the entire Hebrew Bible: 3:16 (woman's desire for man), 4:7 (sin's desire for Cain), and Song 7:10 (lover's desire). The deliberate structural parallel between 3:16 and 4:7 — desire + rule — is preserved in all translations and the consensus.

**Sub-crux D: LXX restructuring**

| Witness | Key differences from MT |
|---|---|
| MT | Sin crouching at the door; broken syntax preserved |
| LXX Vaticanus | No crouching-sin image; "if you offer rightly but do not divide rightly, you have sinned"; adds *ἡσύχασον* ("Be still"); smooths syntax entirely |

The LXX's restructuring almost certainly reflects interpretive response to the broken MT syntax rather than a different Vorlage. The MT is followed in all agents and the consensus. The LXX divergence is the most extensive single-verse divergence in chapter 4 between the MT and Vaticanus.

---

### GEN 4:8 — Missing speech

**Hebrew (MT):** וַיֹּאמֶר קַיִן אֶל-הֶבֶל אָחִיו [*no speech content*] וַיְהִי בִּהְיוֹתָם בַּשָּׂדֶה

| Witness | Speech content |
|---|---|
| MT (Leningrad Codex) | Absent — the lacuna is ancient and present in all MT manuscripts |
| LXX Vaticanus | διέλθωμεν εἰς τὸ πεδίον — "Let us go out into the field" |
| Samaritan Pentateuch | נלכה השדה — "Let us go to the field" |
| Peshitta (Syriac) | "Let us go out to the plain" |
| Vulgate (Latin) | *Egrediamur foras* — "Let us go outside" |
| 4QGen-b (DSS) | Fragment does not preserve this verse |

| Agent | Decision |
|---|---|
| EMET | Preserves MT lacuna: "And Qayin said to Hevel his brother" — no speech supplied |
| LOGOS | Preserves MT lacuna: "And Cain spoke to Abel his brother" — no speech supplied |
| PNEUMA | Supplies LXX/SP reading: "Cain said to his brother Abel, 'Let us go out to the field.'" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Preserves MT lacuna** |

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** The supplied speech is almost certainly original — the MT lacuna is the result of haplographic error (a scribe's eye jumping from *achiv* to the following *vayyehi*). The LXX, SP, Peshitta, and Vulgate all preserve what is likely the original text. Nevertheless, the mandate of this translation is to translate the MT as the primary text, with variants in the apparatus. The *lectio difficilior* governs. PNEUMA's decision to fill the gap is a valid dynamic translation choice but belongs to a different mandate than the consensus translation.

---

### GEN 4:13 — *avoni gadol min-neso'*

**Hebrew:** גָּדוֹל עֲוֹנִי מִנְּשֹׂא

**Double ambiguity:**

*Axis 1 — עָוֹן ('avon):*
- **Iniquity / guilt** — the moral state; what Cain did
- **Punishment / consequence** — the judicial outcome; what Cain now faces

*Axis 2 — מִנְּשֹׂא (min-neso', from נשׂא):*
- **To bear / to carry** — physical or emotional burden: "too great to be borne"
- **To forgive / to lift up** (*naso' 'avon* as idiom for forgiveness, cf. Exod 34:7; Num 14:18): "too great to be forgiven"

This produces four possible readings:
1. "My iniquity is too great to be borne"
2. "My iniquity is too great to be forgiven" ← LXX
3. "My punishment is too great to be borne" ← ESV, NIV, consensus
4. "My punishment is too great to be forgiven"

| Agent | Reading 1 (*'avon*) | Reading 2 (*min-neso'*) | Full rendering |
|---|---|---|---|
| EMET | Both (iniquity/punishment) | Both (bear/be-forgiven) | "My iniquity/punishment is too great to bear/be-forgiven" |
| LOGOS | Iniquity (moral) | Bear (physical) | "My iniquity is too great to bear" |
| PNEUMA | Punishment (judicial) | Carry (physical) | "My punishment is too great to carry" |
| LXX Vaticanus | Guilt/charge (*αἰτία*) | Be forgiven (*ἀφεθῆναι*) | "My guilt is too great to be forgiven" |
| Vulgate | Iniquity | Deserve forgiveness | "My iniquity is greater than I deserve to be forgiven" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Punishment** | **Bear** | **"My punishment is too great to bear"** |

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** The dramatic context of vv. 13–14 — Cain immediately arguing the severity of his sentence ("you are driving me from the face of the ground … whoever finds me will kill me") — supports the punishment/complaint reading. The man who answered "Am I my brother's keeper?" is not confessing guilt in the same breath; he is arguing his case. Nevertheless, the Hebrew עָוֹן is genuinely and permanently polyvalent. No single rendering is wholly adequate. The full ambiguity is irreducible.

---

### GEN 4:23–24 — Lamech's Song: the 77 vs. 490 question

**Hebrew (MT):** וְלֶמֶךְ שִׁבְעִים וְשִׁבְעָה ("and Lamech seventy and seven")
**LXX (Vaticanus):** καὶ Λαμεχ ἑβδομηκοντάκις ἑπτά ("and Lamech seventy times seven" = 490)

| Witness | Number | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| MT (Leningrad Codex) | 77 | שִׁבְעִים וְשִׁבְעָה = "seventy and seven" |
| LXX Vaticanus | 490 | ἑβδομηκοντάκις ἑπτά = "seventy times seven" |
| Matthew 18:22 (NT) | Uses LXX formula | Jesus inverts Lamech's number for forgiveness |

| Agent | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "seventy and seven[fold]" |
| LOGOS | "seventy-sevenfold" |
| PNEUMA | "seventy-seven" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"seventy-sevenfold"** (= 77; MT) |

**SOPHIA's note on the Matthew 18:22 intertextuality:** Whether Jesus in Matthew 18:22 is citing the LXX's 490 or the MT's 77 in inverted form, the theological counter-movement is identical: where Lamech measures vengeance in Lamech-sized multiples, Jesus measures forgiveness in the same units. If the LXX's 490 is what Matthew's audience heard, the contrast is sharper arithmetically; if the MT's 77, the allusion operates through the shared vocabulary of escalation rather than the same number. The MT is followed; the LXX variant and the NT echo are noted.

---

### GEN 4:26 — *huchal liqro'* ("it was begun to call" / "it was profaned to call" / "he hoped to call")

**Hebrew:** אָז הוּחַל לִקְרֹא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה

**The root question:** Is הוּחַל (*huchal*) the Hophal passive of —

- **חלל II** (to begin): "it was begun" — the standard MT reading with Masoretic vocalization
- **חלל I** (to profane/defile): "it was profaned" — the minority reading attested in Targumic tradition
- **יחל** (to hope/wait): "he hoped" — the LXX reading (*ἤλπισεν*), presupposing a different root entirely

| Witness | Reading | Theological import |
|---|---|---|
| MT (Leningrad Codex, Masoretic vocalization) | "it was begun" | Beginning of YHWH-worship; hopeful close to Chapter 4 |
| Targum Onqelos | "then the sons of men began to call their idols by the name of YHWH" | Beginning of idolatry; the profanation reading |
| LXX Vaticanus | "this one [Enosh] hoped to call upon the name of the LORD God" | Personal, eschatological hope; different verb (יחל); adds "God" |
| Peshitta | "it was begun to call on the name of the LORD" | Follows MT |
| Vulgate | *coepit invocare nomen Domini* — "he began to call on the name of the LORD" | Follows MT; personal subject added |

| Agent | Rendering | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Then [it was] begun [lit. it was profaned] to call on the name of YHWH" | Both readings flagged; brackets note the profanation alternative |
| LOGOS | "At that time it was begun to call upon the name of the LORD" | Standard MT; impersonal passive |
| PNEUMA | "At that time, people began to call on the name of the LORD" | MT reading; adds "people" as subject |
| **SOPHIA** | **"At that time it was begun to call upon the name of the LORD"** | **MT; impersonal passive; no subject inserted** |

**SOPHIA's assessment of the "profanation" reading:** Targum Onqelos's reading — that the verse marks the beginning of idolatry, not worship — is theologically significant and should not be dismissed as merely tendentious. The same consonants הוחל can, with different vocalization, be read as the Hophal of חלל I. The Masoretes' vowel pointing chose חלל II, and the Masoretic vocalization is the primary text; the profanation reading is not adopted. But SOPHIA notes that the chapter's downward arc — from Cain's murder through Lamech's sword-song — makes the profanation reading contextually coherent in a way that the standard "it was begun" reading resolves too easily. The ambiguity at the chapter's final word is fitting.

**SOPHIA's assessment of the LXX (Vaticanus):** The LXX's "he hoped" presupposes a completely different Hebrew root (יחל, not חלל) and inserts Enosh as a personal subject. This is a significant divergence reflecting either a different Vorlage or the translator's theological preference. The MT passive construction — with its open, unspecified subject — is theologically richer: *who* began to call? The grammar does not say. The chapter ends in prayer, attributed to no one and to everyone.

---

*Disagreement log compiled by SOPHIA (Σοφία). Council: EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA. Primary text: Leningrad Codex B19A. Cross-reference: Codex Vaticanus Vat.gr.1209 (LXX), Targum Onqelos, Samaritan Pentateuch, Peshitta, Vulgate, 4QGen-b (DSS).*
